perturbation.original_text: 1.1. Affiliate shall mean, when used with reference to a Party, any individual or entity directly or indirectly controlling, controlled by or under common control with such Party. For purposes of this definition, "control" means the direct or indirect ownership of at least 50% of the outstanding voting power of a Party, or the right to control the policy decisions of such Party.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.1. Affiliate shall mean, when used with reference to a Party, any individual or entity directly or indirectly associated with, related to, or partnering with such Party. For purposes of this definition, "control" means having any form of business relationship, including but not limited to, strategic alliances, joint ventures, or marketing agreements with a Party.
perturbation.explanation: The modification broadens the definition of "Affiliate" from entities with a specific ownership threshold (at least 50% of voting power) to any entity with a broad range of business relationships. This creates an in-text contradiction with Section 12.4, which restricts assignment of rights without consent. If an entity is now considered an 'Affiliate' simply through a 'marketing agreement', VerticalNet could argue it can transfer rights to this 'Affiliate' without LeadersOnline's consent, contradicting the original intent of Section 12.4, which intended to only permit transfer to entities under direct corporate control.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 12.4. No Assignment. Except as otherwise set forth herein, neither Party shall transfer, assign or cede any rights or delegate any obligations hereunder, in whole or in part, whether voluntarily or by operation of law, without the prior written consent of the other Party, which consent may be withheld at the other Party's reasonable business discretion; provided, however, that VerticalNet may transfer this Agreement without the prior written consent of LeadersOnline to an Affiliate of VerticalNet, or to the surviving Party in a merger or consolidation, or to a purchaser of all or substantially all of its assets.
perturbation.justification: The changed text broadens the definition of "Affiliate" from entities with a specific ownership threshold (at least 50% of voting power) to any entity with a broad range of business relationships. This directly contradicts Section 12.4, which restricts assignment of rights without consent but allows it to "an Affiliate of VerticalNet". If an entity is now considered an 'Affiliate' simply through a 'marketing agreement', VerticalNet could argue it can transfer rights to this 'Affiliate' without LeadersOnline's consent, contradicting the original intent of Section 12.4, which intended to only permit transfer to entities under direct corporate control.

perturbation.original_text: 6.7. Interest. All payments not paid by the date such payments are due shall bear interest from the due date to the date payments are actually paid at the lower of (a) 1% per month or (b) the maximum rate permitted by law.
perturbation.changed_text: 6.7. Interest. All payments not paid within 30 days of the date such payments are due shall not bear any interest charges whatsoever, unless explicitly stated otherwise in a separate written agreement signed by both parties.
perturbation.explanation: This change creates a direct contradiction. Section 6.7 originally stated that late payments would accrue interest. The modified section states that late payments will not accrue interest unless a separate agreement exists. This creates confusion as to whether interest will be charged on late payments or not. This change will create an in-text contradiction in Section 6.2 [Payment of Fees], on the payment schedule of when payments are due, and since there are payments on the effective date, this means that there could be late fees. In addition, it also changes the agreement made in Section 6.3 [Revenue Sharing], as if LeadersOnline fails to pay on time on the revenue, VerticalNet will have no way to account for the late fees, as the payment does not exist in accordance with this change. This will cause a revenue loss, or have VerticleNet take LeadersOnline to court for non-compliance of paying interest on the late revenue.
perturbation.justification: The modified text in Section 6.7 directly contradicts Section 6.2. Section 6.7 originally stated that late payments would accrue interest, providing a clear expectation of interest on late fees. The changed version states that late payments will not accrue interest unless a separate agreement exists. This conflicts with the payment schedule outlined in Section 6.2, which specifies payment due dates. Since late payments, as defined by missing the dates in 6.2, would now not accrue interest, the expectation set by the original 6.7, and the schedule implied by 6.2 are directly contradicted, making enforcement unclear. The payment schedule in 6.2 could result in late payments, however the changed 6.7 text states that there are no interest charges on the fees in 6.2, creating an in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 7.1. Either Party may terminate this Agreement immediately upon written notice to the other Party in the event of any material breach of a term of this Agreement by such other Party that remains uncured 30 days after notice of such breach was received by such other Party or, if the breach is not reasonably capable of cure within 30 days, such longer period, not to exceed 60 days, so long as the cure is commenced within the 30-day period and thereafter is diligently prosecuted to completion as soon as possible and in any event within 60 days.
perturbation.changed_text: 7.1. Either Party may terminate this Agreement immediately upon written notice to the other Party in the event of any breach of any term of this Agreement by such other Party. No cure period shall be granted. For breaches that are not of material significance, the terminating Party shall notify the breaching Party of the infraction, and termination may proceed immediately after the notification.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause allowed a 30-60 day cure period for material breaches before termination. The modified clause allows immediate termination for *any* breach, regardless of materiality, and eliminates the cure period entirely. This conflicts with Section 8.7, which discusses 'remediable breach' and 'remedial measures', implying that some breaches are curable, directly contradicting the immediate termination provision in the changed Section 7.1. It also creates a contradiction, as the breach is not needed to be material, as any little thing can be grounds for termination now.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 8.7. Remedial Measures. In the event of (a) any material remediable breach of this Agreement by the other Party which remains uncured 30 days after notice of such breach (other than a breach of a payment obligation) was received by the other Party or (b) any material breach which cannot be cured, the non-breaching Party may take reasonable remediable measures upon prior written notice and at the cost and expense of the breaching Party without prejudice and in addition to any other rights arising from such breach. In addition, the non-breaching Party shall take reasonable steps to mitigate damages arising out of such breach.
perturbation.justification: The changed text in Section 7.1, which allows immediate termination for *any* breach and eliminates the cure period, directly contradicts Section 8.7. Section 8.7 explicitly refers to 'material remediable breach' and specifies a 30-day cure period, implying that some breaches are curable. The changed Section 7.1 negates the possibility of a cure period, creating a clear and direct conflict with Section 8.7. This inconsistency would cause confusion and ambiguity regarding the termination rights of each party, thereby creating a strong in-text contradiction.
